coredata.dc:title: Enhancing rate performance of Si@C anode with pore architecture regulated by foaming agent pre-blended with bead mill homogenized Si and graphene

coredata.dc:description: The application of high-capacity silicon (Si) anodes in lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) has attracted considerable attention, although their volume expansion during lithiation/delithiation cycling, unstable solid electrolyte interfacial films, and weak electrical conductivity have diminished their utility in high-energy-density LIBs. Aiming to solve these problems, carbon-coated Si materials with enlarged pore sizes have been synthesized via bead milling in the presence of a foaming agent, sodium dodecyl benzene sulfonate (SDBS). Low-cost and structurally stabled micro-sized Si is homogeneously comminuted and encapsulated synchronously well in as-exfoliated graphene. Besides generating a quantity of carbon quantum dots along the inner surface of the voids, the as-heated foaming agent induces an increase in the Si/ graphene interlayer distance and an enlargement of pores among Si particles. The mesopores provide a buffer space for the volume expansion of Si, while shortening significantly the electrolyte/ion diffusion path, accelerating the diffusion kinetics of lithium ions, and generating abundant electrochemically active sites, which leads to the excellent lithium storage performance of Si@C anode materials. Possessing an adequate porous architecture, SG-S2.5, an anode material fabricated with the initial SDBS dosage of 2.5 %, maintains a capacity of 1270.1 mAh g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1 after 300 cycles with an initial Coulombic efficiency of 89.8 %. When cycled at high current stability (3 A g−1), it exhibits as well considerably sufficient reversible capacity of 934.5 mAh g−1.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Particle size versus milling time curves of Si/GO particles (a), N2 adsorption/desorption isotherm (b) and BIH pore size distribution curves (c) of SG-S0, SG-S2.5 and SG-S10, and SEM images of simple mixed Si and GO (d and e), mSG-S0 (f and g) and mSG-S2.5 (h and i).
SEM images of SG-S0 (a), SG-S1.25 (b), SG-S2.5 (c and d), SG-S5 (e and f) and SG-S10 (g and h).

XRD patterns and FTIR spectra of Si/GO and their composites with different SDBS introductions, including XRD patterns of pSi, SG-S0, SG-S1.25, SG-S2.5, SG-S5 and SG-S10 (a), FTIR spectra of mSG-S0, SG-S0, mSG-S2.5 and SG-S2.5 (b), zoomed-in FTIR spectra (c) and Raman spectra(d) of SG-S0, SG-S1.25, SG-S2.5, SG-S5 and SG-S10, and TG-DSC curves of SDBS (e) and mSG-S2.5 (f).
TEM images of SG-S0 (a), SG-S2.5 (c) and HRTEM images of SG-S0 (b), SG-S2.5 (d) and SDBS-A (e-g).
Deconvoluted XPS peaks for Si2p (a), C1s (b), O1s (c) of mSG-S0, SG-S0, mSG-S2.5 and SG-S2.5.

Cyclic performance of SG-S0, SG-S1.25, SG-S2.5, SG-S5, SG-S10 and SG-S15 at current densities of 1 A g−1 (a) and 3 A g−1 (b), rate performance of SG-S0, SG-S2.5 and SG-S10 (c), constant current charge/discharge curves of SG-S0 (d) and SG-S2.5 (e) at 1 A g−1, a comparison on EIS plots and fitted EIS curves of SG-S0, SG-S1.25, SG-S2.5, SG-S5, SG-S10 and SG-S15 electrodes (f).

CV characteristic curves of SG-S0 (a) and SG-S2.5 (b) measured over the potential range of 0.01–1.5 V at a scan rate of 0.1 mV s−1 for the first to sixth cycles, CV curves of SG-S2.5 at scan rates of 0.2–3.0 mV s−1 (c), log(i) vs log(v) profile (d), the ratios of capacitive contribution and diffusion-controlled contribution at various scan rates (e) and the detailed capacitive contribution at 1.0 mV s−1 (f).
SEM images of cross-sectional morphology of SG-S0, SG-S2.5 and SG-S10 electrode before cycling (a, c and e) and after the 100th cycles (b, d and f).
Schematic illustration of preparation processes for SG-Sx composites.

head.ce:title: Enhancing rate performance of Si@C anode with pore architecture regulated by foaming agent pre-blended with bead mill homogenized Si and graphene

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The application of high-capacity silicon (Si) anodes in lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) has attracted considerable attention, although their volume expansion during lithiation/delithiation cycling, unstable solid electrolyte interfacial films, and weak electrical conductivity have diminished their utility in high-energy-density LIBs. Aiming to solve these problems, carbon-coated Si materials with enlarged pore sizes have been synthesized via bead milling in the presence of a foaming agent, sodium dodecyl benzene sulfonate (SDBS). Low-cost and structurally stabled micro-sized Si is homogeneously comminuted and encapsulated synchronously well in as-exfoliated graphene. Besides generating a quantity of carbon quantum dots along the inner surface of the voids, the as-heated foaming agent induces an increase in the Si/ graphene interlayer distance and an enlargement of pores among Si particles. The mesopores provide a buffer space for the volume expansion of Si, while shortening significantly the electrolyte/ion diffusion path, accelerating the diffusion kinetics of lithium ions, and generating abundant electrochemically active sites, which leads to the excellent lithium storage performance of Si@C anode materials. Possessing an adequate porous architecture, SG-S2.5, an anode material fabricated with the initial SDBS dosage of 2.5 %, maintains a capacity of 1270.1 mAh g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1 after 300 cycles with an initial Coulombic efficiency of 89.8 %. When cycled at high current stability (3 A g−1), it exhibits as well considerably sufficient reversible capacity of 934.5 mAh g−1.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Possessing superior theoretical capacity (4200 mAh g−1), low operating potential (<0.5 V vs Li/Li+), eco-friendliness and low cost, silicon (Si) has been upheld as a promising anode material for lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) [1–3]. Nevertheless, owing to its poor intrinsic ionic/electrical conductivity, the diffusion kinetics of Li+ ions and electrons are limited. Moreover, the severe volume expansion over extended lithiation/delithiation cycles may result into the pulverization of electrode materials, the weakening of electrical contact with current collector and the gradual growth of solid-electrolyte interface (SEI) films, which in turn lead to cumulative losses of active materials [4–6]. Furthermore, inevitable interfacial side-reactions drive repeated destruction-regeneration of SEI films and prompt irreversible capacity loss [7–9]. For these reasons, when used as anode material, Si exhibits rapid capacity decay and poor cycling performance, which hinders its practical application.

To address aforementioned issues, strategies such as nanocrystallization [10,11], surface coating [12,13] and material compositing [14] have been implemented. It has been generally acknowledged that, with high surface/volume ratios, Si particles minimized to nanoscale can withstand significant mechanical stress caused by volume changes, reduce crack propagation and improve structural stability. Unfortunately, nano-Si powder is comparatively expensive to sustain its commercial applications in LIBs. Besides, higher specific surface area for nano-Si may derive particle agglomeration and more electrolyte adsorptions/reactions thereon, resulting in a lower initial coulombic efficiency (ICE).

In contrast, micro-sized Si has the advantages such as high tap density, low cost, high structural stability and facile commercial-scale production, which makes it attractive and admissible for industrial utilization [14–17]. However, in common with nano-Si, the main difficulty in promoting the application of (sub)micro-Si anode is to construct effectively sustainable structures to counteract volume expansion and ionic diffusion inefficiency. Among them, the multiphase compositing or cladding structures have been intensively investigated. In these practices, Si particles are commonly dispersed into or coated by some reactive/inactive materials such as conductive carbon materials like graphene [18,19], amorphous carbon [20], carbon nanotube [1], etc. Therewith, the internal mechanical stress can be relieved to speed up the electrode kinetics during electrochemical reactions, compensate for the lack of conductivity and the buffer volume change [19,21]. Nonetheless, lacking of effective electrolyte diffusion channels, micron-sized Si/graphene anodes fabricated via conventional methods commonly display slow lithium-ion transport, therewith, the cycling performance can hardly be improved as expected.

Porous architectures have attracted widespread attention, as it has been reported that materials with larger pore sizes and thinner shells exhibit better capacity retention. The internal voids can effectively accommodate volume expansion, reduce stress concentration and increase structural stability, meanwhile, the micro- or nano- structures and spacious pores can provide a continuous path for ions and electrons to pass through the electrolyte and the active material. Therewith, energy can be efficiently stored and released, which meets the needs of energy transfer and storage of electrode materials [22–24]. To construct porous Si@C composite structures, various methods have been developed to improve reversibility and cycling stability, including etching [25,26], gas-solid reactions [27], magnesium thermal reduction [28–30], electrophoretic deposition [31], template-assisted synthesis [32] and covalent organic frameworks (COFs) [33]. However, these approaches can hardly to scale up, owing to the use of toxic/expensive materials and/or the complex assembly processes. High-energy ball milling is a facile, fast and cost-effective green technology that can ensure uniform particle distribution and solid-phase synthesis at room temperature [5,27,34]. Despite these advantages, comminuting can disrupt the crystallinity of Si particles, resulting in poor morphology control and limited combination of chemical properties, leading to adverse electrochemical side reactions during charging/discharging processes, affecting battery capacity and cycling stability [25,35,36]. Moreover, the preparation process for Si@C porous structures is essentially complex and delicate, which hinders the large-scale production.

Herein, a simple and scalable method is designed to prepare Si@C composites with large pore size and thin carbon walls through ball milling strategy combined with the introduction of sodium dodecyl benzene sulfonate (SDBS), a foaming agent. Low-cost bulk Si is used in this work as raw material, and high-energy bead milling is used to crush Si particles and exfoliate graphene sheets simultaneously, and then, different concentrations of SDBS were blended in Si@C composites. The micro-sized porous structure of Si-based anode forms conductive and electrolyte diffusion channels, which are conducive to improving the electron and Li+ kinetics, thereby enhancing the rate performance of Si anode. The internal pores, the as-derived carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and the external carbon layer mitigate the volumetric expansion of Si during lithiation process. Additionally, the small specific surface area of micro-sized particles inhibits the formation of SEI films, therewith reducing irreversible capacity loss.

A pristine Si (pSi) powder (purity≥99.9 %) with the nominal average particle diameter of 6 μm from Shanghai ChaoWei Nanotechnology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China) and a graphene oxide (GO, single-layer sheet diameter: 0.2–10 μm, carbon content:~46 wt%) powder from Ashine Advanced Carbon Material Co., Ltd. (Changzhou, China) were used to fabricate Si@C composites. SDBS (C18H29NaO3S, analytic reagent, Sinopharm Group Chemical reagent Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China) was used as the foaming agent to regulate internal pore structure. The flowsheet is illustrated in Scheme 1 . The micron-Si (3 g) and GO (0.3 g) powders were mixed into 100 mL deionized water (resistance≥18.2 MΩ at 25 °C), sonicated for 10 min and then subjected to high-energy comminution for 2.5 h using bead mill Netzsch-MiniCer (NETZSCH-Gerätebau GmbH) with ZrO2 bead diameter of 0.6 mm and bead mill rotation speed of 1500 rpm. The solid product was separated from the slurry by high-speed centrifugation at a relative centrifugal force of 1.1 × 104 g and was rinsed for three times using deionized water, and then vacuum dried at 60 °C for 12 h. The dried powders (mSG-S0, 0.3 g per batch) were added into SDBS solutions (20 mL per batch) of different concentrations (1.25 %, 2.5 %, 5 %, 10 % and 15 %), and the mixtures were stirred, centrifugated and dried. These mSG-Sx samples blended with SDBS are labeled sequentially as mSG-S1.25, mSG-S2.5, mSG-S5, mSG-S10 and mSG-S15. The obtained powders were transferred to a tube furnace for carbonization where the argon (Ar) gas flow rate was maintained at 180 mL cm−1, the temperature was increased from 20 °C to 850 °C at a ramp rate of 10 °C min−1, annealed at 850 °C for 2 h, and then cooled down to ambient temperature to obtain SG-Sx composite samples which were named according to the concentration of pre-blended SDBS as SG-S0, SG-S1.25, SG-S2.5, SG-S5, SG-S10 and SG-S15.

Aiming to investigate the influence of heating process on its composition and structure, the pristine SDBS was annealed in Ar atmosphere following exactly the same temperature-programmed treatment in preparing SG-Sx samples. This product collected from the thermal treatment was labeled as SDBS-A.

The particle size distribution of micro-Si/GO composites was ascertained using a laser particle analyzer (BT-9300ST, Dandong Bettersize Instruments Ltd.). Their crystal structure was analyzed by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD, diffractor TD-3500, Dandong Tongda Technology Co., Ltd.), using Cukα radiation, generated at tube current 30 mA and tube voltage 35 kV, as the X-ray source. Nitrogen (N2) adsorption/desorption isotherms were performed using an ASAP 2460 (Micromeritics Instruments) and the specific surface area was calculated by Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) and pore size distribution by Barret-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) method. The surface topography and microstructure were observed by using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, JSM-7900F, JEOL) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-F200, JEOL). Using an Octane Elect Super EDS system (EDAX) and an Ultim Max 80 (Oxford Instruments), the energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) was applied to analyze the element content and distribution. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed on an Escalab XI+ (Thermo Fisher Scientific) system using Alkα radiation (hν = 1486.6 eV) to characterize the surface chemistry. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet iS50) was used to collect spectra over 4000–400 cm−1 to study the surface functional groups. Raman spectra over 0–3500 cm−1 were recorded using a laser confocal micro-Raman spectrometer inVia Qontor (Renishaw) with Ar+ laser (λ = 532 nm) as the light source. Using TGA/DSC1 (Mettler Toledo), thermalgravimetric (TG) analysis and differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) were operated from 30 to 900 °C with a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 under Ar atmosphere.

The working electrode, which is uniformly coated on copper foil, consists of 60 % active substance, 20 % Ketjen black (carbon, Lion Corporation) as the conductive agent and 20 % sodium alginate ((C6H7O6Na)n, Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute) as the binder, and is compacted under 20 MPa. The loading amount of the active substance on the electrode is about 0.8–1.2 mg cm−2. The CR2025 coin-type half cells were assembled with lithium foil as the counter electrode, Celgard 2400 polypropylene membrane as the separator, and placed in an Ar-filled glove box (LG1200/750TS, Vigor, Gas Purification Technologies (Suzhou) Co., Ltd.) with water and oxygen adjusted to ≤0.01 ppm. The electrolyte was compounded by mixing LiPF6 (1 M) pre-dissolved in a mixture of diethyl carbonate (DEC)/ethylene carbonate (EC) (DEC: EC = 1:1 W/W, 89 vol%), vinyl carbonate (VC, 1 vol%) and fluoroethylene carbonate (FEC, 10 vol%). Cyclic voltammetry (CV) analysis was conducted using a Potentiostat/Gavanostat interface 1010 system (Gamry Instruments) with a voltage range of 0.01–1.5 V and a scan rate of 0.1–1.5 mV s−1. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed on an electrochemical workstation CHI660A (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co., Ltd.) with a frequency range of 10−1–105 Hz.

As shown in Fig. 1 a, the size of the composite sample Si/GO can be rapidly reduced by bead milling. After 150 min of milling, the average particle size (D50) is reduced to 0.752 μm. From the particle size distribution plot (Fig. S1), it can be found that the Si/GO particles are more uniformly distributed and the majority of the particles (about 67 %) are <1 μm.

The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and mesopore size distributions of thermal treated samples, SG-S0, SG-S2.5 and SG-S10 are shown in Figs. 1 b and c, respectively, while those of SG-S1.25 and SG-S5 are presented in Fig. S2. Apparently, the foaming agent SDBS can induce stepwise an annexation of small pores and enlarge pore space among Si particles and between Si and graphene. This results in a gradual reduction in the BET specific surface areas, as those for SG-S0, SG-S1.25, SG-S2.5, SG-S5 and SG-S10 are 48.18, 43.64, 38.72, 37.76 and 36.63 m2 g−1, respectively. The adsorption/desorption curves have strong adsorption and significant H3-type hysteresis over the low-pressure region. These verify further the existence of micropores and mesopores, while the isotherms are type IV isotherms. Compared with SG-S0, SG-S2.5 exhibits an improved adsorption capacity over the low relative pressure range, while the adsorption on SG-S10 declines. Abundant mesopores have unique structural advantages to facilitating electrolyte penetration and Li+ diffusion [37,38]. They also help to mitigate the volume change of active Si particles during lithium insertion to obtain excellent cycling stability. The pore size distribution curves ( Fig. 1 c) show that, the pores are mainly of two ranges, ~4 nm and ~ 52 nm, while the whole pore volume descends with the increasing SDBS dosage, testifying the decline of small pores. The rational specific surface area of SG-S2.5 favors electrolyte penetration and enhancement of coulombic efficiency (CE) by reducing the interface for direct contacted Si and electrolyte. This, in turn, mitigates the likelihood of side reactions.

The effects of bead milling can be evaluated by comparing the morphology and size. For the simply blended pristine Si and GO ( Fig. 1 d and Fig. S3a), the majority of unmilled Si particles with size ranging from a few micrometers to tens of micrometers show an irregular shape with sharp edges. Meanwhile, raw GO ( Fig. 1 e) presents the stacked shape and remains randomly dispersed. For bead milled samples (mSG-S0 and mSG-S2.5), as shown in Figs. 1 f-i and Figs. S3b-c, Si particles show a narrowed particle size distribution and a homogenized shape as the acute angle becomes less pronounced. Meanwhile, the sliced and thinned GO sheets can attach and spread firmly onto Si clusters [39,40]. When subjected to expansion during lithiation, Si particles with duller surfaces tend to be subjected to isotropic mechanical stresses rather than anisotropic stresses. With this structure, Si particles are well covered by thin GO layers, it is expected that a conductive network and buffering substrate may be realized for Li−Si reaction.

To observe the pore-forming effect of SDBS, the microstructures of samples with varied SDBS content are explored. SEM images of SG-S0, SG-S1.25, SG-S2.5, SG-S5 and SG-S10 are shown in Fig. 2
. With the change of SDBS mass fraction, the morphology of these Si@C composites varies significantly. When no SDBS has been added in the as-blended composite, Si particles in sample SG-S0 ( Fig. 2 a and Fig. S4a) pack tightly. Then, as can be seen from Fig. 2 b and Fig. S4b, a small number of gaps appears between Si particles in SG-S1.25, showing the slight effect of pore forming of a small dosage of SDBS. When the mass faction of SDBS attains to 2.5 % (SG-S2.5), abundant and homogeneous pores appear ( Fig. 2 c and Fig. S4c). This well-developed pore structure may favor the mitigation and adaptation of volume changes throughout the whole electrode and reduce the fragmentation of Si clusters, guaranteeing the excellent mechanical integrity [41]. The zoomed-in image ( Fig. 2 d) shows that Si particles are encapsulated with a layer of filamentous flower-like graphene deposits. This structure can provide short transport channels for electron and ion transport, thus conferring excellent electrical conductivity throughout the electrode [42]. The EDS mapping analysis (Fig. S5) is conducted to investigate the spatial distribution Si and C. Their uniform distribution confirms the homogeneous carbon layer coating on Si particles. On the Si@C composites with larger SDBS mass fractions, SG-S5 ( Figs. 2 e and f and Fig. S4d) and SG-S10 ( Figs. 2 g and h and Figs. S4e and f), obvious cracks appear. The cracks on SG-S5 are small and the surface is still covered with a folded graphene layer. In contrast, the particles in SG-S10 are loosely split, and there seems no thin carbon layer adjacent to the cracks, indicating the destroy of the overall Si/carbon structure by overdosed SDBS.

Diffraction peaks of Si crystals can be observed on the XRD patterns of the raw Si powder, pSi, and Si/C composite samples ( Fig. 3 a and Fig. S6). The peaks for original Si powder are sharp and strong, showing that pSi processes perfect crystallinity. The peak intensity of pulverized Si reduces remarkably, testifying the size reducing and defect derivation result. All Si@C samples exhibit five peaks at 28.5°, 47.4°, 56.2°, 69.2° and 76.4° that correlate with (111), (220), (311), (400) and (331) planes of face-centered cubic-structured Si (JCPDS #27–1402). There is no observable peak shift on XRD patterns of mSG-Sx and SG-Sx samples, while the relative peak intensity also sustains. This indicates that the crystalline Si in the composites maintains its original lattice structure during the annealing treatment. The peak emerged at ca. 22° corresponds to the (002) crystal plane of CQDs (Fig.S6b) [43]. In comparison to the other five samples, the (002) peak intensity of SG-S15 is significantly enhanced, indicating a higher content of CQDs. The characteristic diffraction peak of (001) at ca. 10° for original GO is not indetectable [44]. This is probably due to the adequate exfoliation of graphene flakes amidst ball milling and it prevents the formation of an ordered stacked structure, which has been confirmed by the aforediscussed SEM visualizations.

The FTIR spectrum of SDBS manifests typical vibrational modes (Fig. S7 and Table S1) like antisymmetric stretching vibration of C=C (ν as C=C, 1647 cm−1) [45], bending vibration of −CH2 (δ −CH2 , 1459 cm−1), asymmetric stretching of S=O (ν as S=O, epoxy, 1182 cm−1), C−C skeleton vibration (1126 cm−1), antisymmetric stretching vibration of S−O (ν as S−O, 1135 cm−1), stretching vibration of hydroxyl (C−OH) group (ν s C−OH, 1039 cm−1) [46], in-plane bending vibration of C−H (δ −CH3, 1011 cm−1), etc. On the spectrum of SDBS-A (Fig. S7), the disappearance of sulfur-containing species may be attributed to the dissipation of sulfide gas at elevated temperatures. On profiles of mSG-S0, SG-S0, mSG-S2.5 and SG-S2.5 ( Fig. 3 b), the peaks centered at 471 cm−1 correspond to Si−O bending vibrations, while the strong bands over 890–1250 cm−1 correspond to asymmetric stretching vibrations of Si−O−Si or Si−O−C [47]. For mSG-Sx, Si dangling bonds generated via bead milling can react with −OH radicals to form Si−OH bonds, which eventually evolve into Si−O−Si bonds or combine with C−OH groups on GO to form Si−O−C bonds. Meanwhile, for thermal treated samples (SG-Sx), the ν s Si−OH peaks located at ~878 cm−1 [45] disappear (Figs. S8 and S9), suggesting the reaction of hydroxyl species amidst thermal treatment. Consistently with this, the strength of O−H bands at ~3200 cm−1 originating from carboxylic acid groups in GO and SDBS reduces. These changes indicates a successful coupling of Si and rGO. As shown in Fig. 3c, with the increase of SDBS content, the ν s Si−O−Si peak centered at ~789 cm−1 [40] enhances significantly, which may be attributed to the formation of Si−O−C and Si−O−S bonds resulted from the cleavage of residual SDBS and the increase of carbon content during thermal reduction. In this case, Si−O−Si/Si−O−C/Si−O−S bands shift toward high wavenumbers range. Moreover, because the as-formed Si−O−C and C−O−S have higher absorption frequencies than Si−O−Si, a blue-shift for the broad band located at 890–1250 cm−1 can as well be observed.

Raman spectra are used to compare the of graphitization degree of different samples. For all the Si@C structures, there appear on the Raman spectra ( Fig. 3 d and Fig. S10) prominent peaks at ca. 520 and 950 cm−1 [45], which correspond respectively to the first-order scattering of Si−Si stretching vibration and the second-order scattering of the 2TO-phonon mode of Si [48,49]. Two typical carbon peaks, D peak and G peak, can be detected at ca. 1350 cm−1 and 590 cm−1. The D peak is associated with sp3 hybridization, reflecting the degree of disorder in the crystal structure, while the G peak corresponds to first-order scattering from the E2g vibrational mode [15,35]. The ID/IG values for SG-S0, SG-S1.25, SG-S2.5, SG-S5 and SG-S10 are 1.30, 1.10, 1.03, 1.19 and 1.34, respectively. Possessing the highest graphitization degree, SG-S2.5 exhibits an optimized electrical conductivity, which facilitates Li+ transport therein.

There are three stages of weight loss on the TG-DSC curves of SDBS ( Fig. 3 e), labeled as stage I (before 300 °C), stage II (between 300 and 500 °C) and stage III (after 500 °C). Before 300 °C, SDBS loses about 8.0 % of its weight due to the removal of adsorbed water and other volatiles. Over stage II, SDBS undergoes a significant weight loss of about 53.8 %, which is mainly attributed to the cleavage of molecular chains as well as the volatilization of the alkyl chains. As the temperature increases (stage III), the molecular chains of SDBS carbonize further, and a continued weight loss occurs. The TG curve for mSG-S2.5 ( Fig. 3 f) can be divided roughly into two stages. The stage with a total weight loss of 9.0 % can be attributed into an initial weight loss before 200 °C caused by the desorption of adsorbed water and volatiles, a deoxygenation reaction and graphitization of GO over 200–600 °C, and the decomposition of SDBS [50,51]. Over the high temperature interval of 600–900 °C, the deoxygenation of GO is almost complete [52]. The weight gain of 5.2 % over the second stage may be due to adsorption of sulfur-containing compounds generated by SDBS decomposition or chemical reactions to derive sulfur-containing oxides, as well as the possibility of Si oxidation at elevated temperatures.

Important changes in morphology and microstructure of SG-S0, SG-S2.5 and SG-S10 are further observed by TEM images. From Fig. S11a, it is evident that, overall, SG-S0 is tightly packed, with a particularly severe pile-up in the center. The pristine holes in SG-S0 enlarge ( Fig. 4 a) and the periphery of the holes is shot for high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) graphs ( Fig. 4 b and Fig. S12a), which shows that Si particles are covered by thin rGO layers and have a smooth surface [6,7]. The TEM image of SG-S2.5 is shown in Fig. S11b, where homogeneous pore structures appeared relative to SG-S0, and these pores not only relieve the volume expansion of Si particles and enhance the structural integrity and cycling performance, but also promote electrolyte penetration, which contributes to the rate capability and transfer kinetics of Li+. With overdosed SDBS, cracked and loosed structure can be observed (Fig. S11c). As illustrated in Fig. S11d, Si particles in SG-S2.5 constitute a homogeneous internal framework, while, as shown in the EDS layered image (Fig. S11e) and EDS spectrum (Fig. S11f), thin C layer, with the mass ratio of only 5.6 %, forms an external cladding layer. This configuration serves to enhance the conductivity of the material and increase the surface active sites. Figs. 4 c and d and Fig. S12b, where the latter is an enlarged image of the rectangular region in Fig. 4 d, show the rough surface of the outer layer of the internal holes, which appears as granular deposits, forming a thin layer, which contributes to ensure excellent conductivity and stabilizing the SEI films [41,42]. Notably, HRTEM images confirm the crystallinity of Si with lattice spacings of 0.16, 0.19 and 0.31 nm, respectively, related to (311), (220) and (111) crystallographic planes of Si. To analyze the morphological and structural changes of SDBS, HRTEM images of SDBS-A are presented in Figs. 4 e-g and Figs. S12c and d. After thermal treatment, rough surface and some black particles appear in SDBS-A. These black particles are well dispersed, non-agglomerated and nearly monodisperse, with a size range of 2–5 nm, corresponding to those in the box and circle in Fig. 4 d. An HRTEM image ( Fig. 4 f) of a single particle with an interplanar spacing estimate of 0.18 nm, which can be assigned to the (102) graphite plane, verifying the formation of CQDs. The lattice spacing of 0.24 nm corresponds to the (002) plane of Na2SO4, a crystallite product of SDBS annealing. Compared with those in SG-S0 and SG-S2.5, pores in SG-S10 are further enlarged and the overall structure is cracked (Fig. S13), which builds an unstable void space.

The peaks centered at 284.8, 286.4 and 288.8 eV on the high resolution C1s XPS spectra of SDBS-A (Fig. S14a) corresponds respectively to C−C/C=C, C−O and C=O [53], while the O1s spectrum (Fig. S14b) can be fitted into two peaks at 531.3 eV and 535.7 eV, which can be assigned to S=O and C−O. The deconvoluted Si 2p, O1s and C1s XPS spectra are shown sequentially in Figs. 5 a-c, while the XPS survey spectra of mSG-S0, SG-S0, mSG-S2.5 and SG-S2.5 are displayed in Fig. S15. For mSG-S0 and mSG-S2.5, the major chemical states of Si include Si−Si (99.5 eV) [45], Si−O−C (102.3 eV), Si−OH (103.0 eV) and Si−O−Si (103.6 eV) [45]. For SG-S0 and SG-S2.5, the intensity of Si−Si peak decreases significantly, which may be attributed to the weakening of photoelectron yield of Si due to GO wrapping amidst thermal reduction. The disappearance of Si−OH may be caused by evaporation during thermal reduction or by forming Si−O−Si.

There are C−C/C=C (284.6 eV), Si−O−C (285.3 eV), C−OH (286.4 eV), C−O−C (287.2 eV) and −COOH (288.4 eV) peaks [54–56] on C 1 s spectra of mSG-S0 and mSG-S2.5, which verifies the presence of GO and its interaction with freshly cleaved Si grains. After thermal reduction, C−C/C=C peak in SG-S2.5 slides toward the low binding energy side, suggesting the cleavage of C=C [57,58]. In addition, the peak at 288.4 eV shifts to higher binding energy, but this phenomenon does not occur in SG-S0, which may be due to the thermal decomposition of SDBS which leads to the reaction of S=O with C−O=C or Si−OH to produce Si−O−S or C−O−S.

The main chemical states of O on the O1s spectra of mSG-S0 and mSG-S2.5 are C−O−C (531.3 eV), −COOH (531.8 eV), Si−OH (532.3 eV), Si−O−Si (532.9 eV), Si−O−C and C−OH (533.7 eV) [37,38]. For SG-S0 and SG-S2, the entire O1s spectrum shifts toward higher binding energies, probably due to the weakening of C−O−C due to the thermal disruption and the disappearance of C−O=C and Si−OH peaks. The elevated contents of Si−O−Si and Si−O−C demonstrate the intensifying Si-GO reactions, which is consistent with the discussion on Si2p and C1s XPS spectra and the FTIR analyses. Probably due to the formation of Si−O−S or C−O−S, the position of S2p peak of SG-S2.5 (Fig. S16) shifts toward higher binding energies with respect to mSG-S2.5.

According to the constant current charge/discharge curves of anodes at the current density of 1 A g−1 ( Fig. 6 a), the first two cycles are activated at a current density of 0.1 A g−1. The initial discharge/charge specific capacities of SG-S0, SG-S1.25, SG-S2.5, SG-S5, SG-S10 and SG-S15 are respectively 3063.3/2165.8, 2499.7/2149.2, 3095.0/2778.6, 2741.7/1688.6, 1887.2/1370.6 and 1810.2/1145.9 mAh g−1, with the corresponding ICE of 70.7 %, 86.0 %, 89.8 %, 61.6 %, 72.6 % and 63.3 %. The difference between the initial discharge/charge specific capacities may originated from electrolyte decomposition and SEI layer formation which may result in a substantial capacity decay. After 300 cycles, the reversible capacities of SG-S0, SG-S1.25, SG-S2.5, SG-S5, SG-S10 and SG-S15 are 857.0, 944.7, 1270.1, 818.8, 740.1 and 700.7 mAh g−1, respectively, with the CE close to 100 %. During charge/discharge process, the mechanically strong and flexible graphene layer contributes to the upgraded capacity by protecting the surface of Si particles from reacting excessively with electrolyte, buffering the volume expansion of Si and inhibiting the recurrent growth of SEI. The high CE may be derived from its unique pore structure and highly conductive network.

In order to further investigate the cycling performance of the electrodes, SG-Sx electrodes are tested for 300 cycles at the current density of 3 A g−1 ( Fig. 6 b). Overall, as shown in Fig. S17, we compared the electrochemical performance of separately ball-milled Si (mSi) and found the specific capacities of mSi decline remarkably after 300 cycles at 1 A g−1 and 3 A g−1 to 154.7 mAh g−1 and 56.6 mAh g−1, respectively. The reversible capacities of SG-S0, SG-S1.25, SG-S2.5, SG-S5, SG-S10 and SG-S15 are 722.9, 888.5, 934.5, 724.8, 570.0 and 485.0 mAh g−1, respectively, with the corresponding capacity retention as 44.7 %, 53.9 %, 62.9 %, 52.0 %, 54.9 % and 59.9 %. The incorporation of CQDs has been demonstrated to enhance the electrical conductivity of the composite material, thereby optimizing the electron transport properties within the electrodes. The results show that the reversible capacity of SG-S2.5 electrode is significantly higher than that of other five electrodes, which suggests that the internal porosity and overall structural integrity of SG-S2.5 are favorable for improving cycling stability.

It is noteworthy that the Li+ transport channel of SG-S2.5 is established by introducing a pore-forming agent rather than simply by encapsulating micro-sized Si particles in graphene, which will be further demonstrated in the rate capability ( Fig. 6 c). When the current density is 0.1, 0.5, 1, 2, 3 and 5 A g−1, respectively, the specific discharge capacities of SG-2.5 are 3889.6, 2986.6, 2651.9, 2376.7, 1776.7 and 1382.4 mAh g−1, respectively. It is very clear that the rate performance of SG-S0 and SG-S10 is inferior to that of SG-S2.5. When the current density is restored to 0.5 A g−1, SG-S2.5 regains a reversible capacity of 2695.3 mAh g−1, showing a capacity recovery of 90.2 %. The markedly enhanced electrochemical performance of SG-S2.5 indicates that the extensive pores produced by the pore-generation process can effectively accommodate the volume change of Si during cycling, increase Li+ transport channels and thus greatly improve the cycling performance. Meanwhile, the fact that SG-S0 does not introduce a porogenic agent results in the electrolyte not being able to access carbon-encapsulated Si particles, which reduces the total capacity. The excess pore-forming agent in SG-S10 breaks the overall structure and shows a reduced capacity.

The voltage distribution plots (voltage versus capacity plots) for the 1st, 10th, 50th, 100th and 200th constant current charge/discharge cycles of SG-S0 and SG-S2.5 are shown in Figs. 6 d and e, while those for SG-S1.25 and SG-S5 are displayed respectively in Figs. S18a and b. During the first discharge, the discharge voltage has a low plateau at ~0.2 V, which is related to the alloying of Li+ with Si. The irreversible capacity loss in the first cycle is mainly attributed to the irreversible depletion of lithium caused by the formation of SEI layer on the electrode surface. The discharge plateau after the first cycle of SG-S0 and SG-S2.5 increased by ca. 0.15 V [59,60]. The curves are relatively skewed because the amorphization of crystalline Si during lithiation/delithiation processes [61,62]. In this way, the deposition of lithium metal on anode and the growth of lithium dendrites on anode during the high-current charging process can be reduced or avoided, so as to improve the safety of batteries. What this clearly shows is the contribution of the large pore size in SG-S2.5 to the lithium storage capacity retention and cycling stability.

The kinetic process of its electrochemical reaction is investigated. The EIS plots of SG-Sx electrodes are displayed in Fig. 6 f, with equivalent circuit diagrams shown in the upper left inset. All these plots consist of a semicircle in the high-frequency region and a diagonal line in the low-frequency range, with the semicircle indicating the charge transfer resistance (Rct) and the diagonal line the Warburg impedance corresponding to the diffusion of Li+ ions (W
0 ) [59–61]. Generally, the larger the semicircular diameter of the high frequency region, the higher the corresponding charge transfer impedance. The calculated Rs and Rct are shown in Table S2. Since the electrolyte for the six synthesized samples are the same, no significant difference in their electrolyte resistance (Rs) is observed. The results show that, compared to SG-S0, SG-S1.25, SG-S5, SG-S10 and SG-S15, SG-S2.5 has the smallest Rct (57.17 Ω), suggesting that its enhanced electrical conductivity contributes to the improvement of the reaction kinetics of Li+.

Compared to existing studies with feedstocks of Si and graphene (Fig. S19 and Table S3), SG-S2.5 exhibits excellent electrochemical properties, and in addition, the facile synthesis enables it a viable candidate for industrial-scale production.

The cyclic voltammograms for the 1st to 6th cycles of SG-S0 and SG-S2.5 are shown in Figs. 7 a and b. In the initial cycle, the formation of amorphous LixSi takes place considerably below 0.1 V. For SG-S0, the irreversible peak appeared at ~0.5 V during the first lithiation disappears in subsequent cycles. The weaker reduction peak at 0.5 V for SG-S2.5 indicates the formation of a stable SEI films with less irreversible capacity loss and high ICE, as has been demonstrated in cycling performance. The formation of SEI films is the main cause of the initial irreversible capacity loss. The reduction peaks of SG-S0 and SG-S2.5, corresponding to the alloying process of Si, are centered respectively at 0.18 V and 0.20 V. The oxidation peaks at 0.36 V and 0.52 V relate to the reversion of amorphous Li−Si alloy to crystalline Si [62,63]. The polarization reaction on SG-S2.5 is moderate, as it displays a smaller gap between oxidation and reduction peaks. Furthermore, in the following cycles, the peak intensity increases while the position remains essentially unchanged, indicating that the electrodes are gradually activated. The peak of SG-S2.5 is more powerful than other samples (The CV characteristic curves of SG-S1.25 and SG-S5 are displayed in Figs. S20a and b), representing the best lithiation/delithiation reaction kinetics.

The CV curves of SG-S2.5 electrode at scan rates of 0.2, 0.5, 0.7, 1.0 and 1.5 mV s−1 are shown in Fig. 7 c. When the scan rate increases with the current intensity, the CV curves exhibit similar characteristic redox peaks despite gradual shifting and broadening, indicating the good reversibility of SG-S2.5. Capacitive and diffusion-controlled processes are usually present in the electrode materials of LIBs. The peak current (i) can be detected by changing the CV test scan rate (ν), and their relationship can be described by the following formula:

where і is the peak current, ν is the scan rate, and a and b are constants. In general, when b values 0.5, it suggests that the Li storage via the conversion reaction in the anode is diffusion-controlled, while the b-value of 1.0 suggests a pseudocapacitive-controlled Li storage [64,65]. As depicted in Fig. 7 d, the b-values of peaks A and B of SG-S2.5 are 0.98 and 0.97, respectively, which indicates that the attenuation and lithiation are capacitance-controlled reactions. The contributions of capacitive and diffusion-controlled charge storage can be quantified as:

correspond to the current contributions from surface capacitive effects and diffusion-controlled intercalation processes, respectively [66]. The percentage capacitive contribution for SG-S2.5 grows from 45.8 % to 72.9 % with the scan rate ascending from 0.2 to 3 mV s−1 ( Fig. 7 e) and the lithiation/decay response gradually shifts to diffusion control. The detailed capacitive contribution at 1.0 mV s−1 for SG-S2.5 is presented in Fig. 7 f. This facilitates a more efficient and rapid transfer of Li+, suggesting that SGS-2.5 has better rate performance and cycling stability.

To further investigate the structural stability and integrity of electrodes, the top-view morphology of SG-S0, SG-S2.5 and SG-S10 electrodes before and after charging/discharging cycles at 1 A g−1 is observed by SEM (Fig. S21). Although the surfaces of the assembled anodes are smooth, the electrode shows obvious cracks after 100 cycles. Particularly for SG-S10 electrode, there is extensive chalking. However, there are clearly fewer cracks on the SG-S2.5 electrode. The longitudinal expansion of electrodes can distinctly illustrate their structural evolution [66,67]. As shown in Fig. 8 , from the same thickness of the original electrode layers of about 19.2 mm, SG-S0, SG-S2.5 and SG-S10 electrodes thicken respectively by 20.8 %, 18.2 % and 50 %. The results show that the well-developed mechanical properties of graphene and the abundant pores in SG-S2.5 can buffer crack extension and restrict crack formation, therewith alleviate the electrode cyclic stress and maintain structural stability [68].

From XRD patterns (Fig. S22a) of the cycled SG-S0, SG-S2.5 and SG-S10 electrodes and the assignment of diffraction peaks (Table S4), we can find the derivation of a number of lithium-containing species such as Li2CO3, LiF, Li2O, Li15Si4 and a-LiySi [68]. The Li2CO3 and SiO2 diffraction peaks of SG-S10 are stronger than those of SG-S2.5 (Fig. S22b). Owing to the overdosed SDBS, there are more Si grains exposed on SG-S10 and the SEI films is constantly broken and regenerated whilst cycling, resulting in an increase in the generation of Li2CO3 and SiO2. The suitable pore structure of SG-S2.5 can effectively alleviate the volume effect of Si and improve the stability of SEI film, thereby significantly inhibiting the formation of Li2CO3 and SiO2.

Meanwhile, Li-containing species including Li2CO3, LiOH, ROCO2Li, LiPF6 and ROLi can also be verified by FTIR analyses of the anode after cycling (Fig. S23 and Table S5). The C=C asymmetric stretching vibration of SG-S2.5 remains stable, indicating that the carbon coating structure is relatively intact after cycling. The C−O−C and Si−O vibrations of SG-S0 and SG-S10 are stronger than those of SG-S2.5, suggesting a controled surface oxidation of SG-S2.5 during the cycling process.

By bead milling, the bulk Si and GO are crushed and thinned, and the foaming agent SDBS is added, followed by further thermal reduction to form Si@C composites with homogeneous pores and structural integrity. Without generating pores, the close-packed structure may cause the electrolyte inaccessible to Si particles encapsulated by the carbon layer, resulting in incomplete lithiation process. However, the introduction of excessive SDBS can conversely destroy the structure, therewith, the carbon layer can hardly mitigate the volume expansion of Si, and, there may generate repeatedly SEI films during the cycling process which increases the consumption of electrolyte. By controlling SDBS content, the pore structure can be well constructed. The effective protection of rGO, the uniformity of the pores and the structural integrity can effectively suppress the volume change of Si during cycling, and also provides sufficient ion transport paths while enhancing the cycling stability and rate retention. After cycled for 300 cycles at a current density of 1 A g−1, SG-S2.5, composited with the initial SDBS dosage of 2.5 %, possesses a reversible capacity of 1229.7 mAh g−1, which is 43.5 % higher than SG-S0, the counterpart without pore-creating. Furthermore, SG-S2.5 exhibits excellent cycling performance by maintaining a reversible capacity of 934.5 mAh g−1 even at high current density (3 A g−1).
